items.q: What is the cosmic microwave background?
items.correct: Relic radiation from when the universe became transparent, about 380,000 years after the Big Bang.
items.misconception: Background noise from human microwave ovens.
items.random_wrong: Light from the most distant galaxies.
items.why: Students take 'microwave' as the appliance. The CMB is photons from the early universe, now stretched to microwave wavelengths by expansion - the earliest light we can detect, predicted by Big Bang theory.

items.q: Why do astronomers infer the existence of dark matter?
items.correct: Visible matter alone cannot account for galaxy rotation curves and gravitational lensing.
items.misconception: Dark matter has been directly observed in telescopes.
items.random_wrong: Because the universe looks dark at night.
items.why: Students think dark matter has been seen. It's INFERRED from gravitational effects on visible matter and light - galaxies rotate too fast for visible mass alone, implying unseen matter contributes most gravity.

items.q: What does a telescope's aperture primarily determine?
items.why: Students think bigger telescopes magnify more. Magnification depends on eyepiece. Aperture determines how much light you collect (faint objects visible) and resolution (fine detail visible). Big aperture = big telescope.

items.q: Who formulated the law of universal gravitation?
items.why: It's Newton. “Kepler” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Planets sweep equal areas in equal times is which law?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Second. “First” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximate altitude of geostationary orbit in thousands of km?
items.why: Exact answer: 36 thousand km. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
